1. Polymarket: Shocks vs News
  1. Dashboard
  2. Methodology

Polymarket: who moves first, prices or news?

For each of high-impact shocks on Polymarket (large probability jumps with serious money behind them) we measured the timestamp gap to the nearest substantive Wikipedia edit on the relevant article. After excluding novelty markets (tweet counts, etc.) and scheduled-event markets (elections during exit polls, awards ceremonies, live floor votes, etc.) — where the price-vs-Wikipedia comparison is unreliable by construction — remain.

The median remaining shock leads Wikipedia by .

How decisive is the lead?

Decisive: shock first

of matched shocks

Price moved more than 3 hours before the Wikipedia article was edited. Hardest to explain by "slow Wikipedia editor."

Within ±30 min

of matched shocks

Inside the detector's own 1-hour resolution. We cannot adjudicate these without sub-hour shock timing or finer-grained news data.

Decisive: news first

of matched shocks

Wikipedia was edited more than 3 hours before the price moved. The cleanest "market reacted to public news" cases.

Shock first, marginal (30 min – 3 h) of matched
News first, marginal (30 min – 3 h) of matched
No Wikipedia edit in ±7 d of substantive shocks

Lead/lag distribution and category mix

Exemplars — top cases for each band

Audit: cases we filtered out as scheduled-event spurious

These shocks were excluded from the headline counts above because the question text or Wikipedia article suggests a public-but-sub-Wikipedia information feed (exit polls, live award ceremony, live floor vote, year-end search rankings, etc.). Inspect the heuristic in the methodology.

Methodology
Source: Polymarket on-chain trades (Nov 2022 – Mar 2026) via vgregoire/polymarket-users; news: Wikipedia revisions (GDELT to follow).
